BusBrother offers direct group shuttle service from any Orlando area pickup point.

sections.content: On-site parking at Port Canaveral runs about $17 per day for open lots and $22 per day for covered garages. BusBrother group shuttle service eliminates parking costs and drops your group directly at the terminal entrance with luggage assistance.

title: Orlando International Airport (MCO) - Complete Guide
subtitle: Everything you need to know about Orlando International Airport - terminals, airlines, ground transportation, parking, and group shuttle options.

sections.heading: About Orlando International Airport
sections.content: Orlando International Airport (MCO) is the busiest airport in Florida and one of the top 10 busiest in the United States, serving over 57 million passengers annually. The airport is located about 12 miles southeast of downtown Orlando. MCO serves as the primary gateway for visitors to Walt Disney World, Universal Orlando, SeaWorld, and other Central Florida attractions. The airport code MCO comes from its former name, McCoy Air Force Base.

sections.heading: Terminal Layout
sections.content: MCO has two main terminals connected by a people mover system. Terminal A and Terminal B are each divided into four gate sections called airsides (1 through 4). The main terminal building houses ticketing, baggage claim, and the food court. A new Terminal C opened in 2022, adding 15 gates and serving primarily international and domestic carriers including JetBlue and Caribbean airlines.

sections.heading: Airlines and Routes
sections.content: Over 50 airlines operate at MCO, including Southwest, JetBlue, Delta, American, United, Spirit, Frontier, and international carriers like British Airways and Lufthansa. Southwest is the largest carrier at MCO by passenger volume. The airport offers nonstop flights to over 150 domestic and 50 international destinations.

sections.heading: Ground Transportation
sections.content: MCO offers multiple ground transportation options including rental cars, rideshare pickup on Level 2 of the terminal garages, taxis, hotel shuttles, and the Brightline train connection. BusBrother provides group shuttle and charter bus service directly from MCO baggage claim to any Central Florida destination.

sections.heading: Parking Options
sections.content: MCO offers several parking options ranging from economy lots at $10 per day to the terminal top parking garage at $27 per day. The North and South parking garages are connected directly to the terminals. Economy parking includes a shuttle to the terminal. For groups, BusBrother pickup at the terminal eliminates parking costs entirely.

title: MCO Terminals Guide - Navigating Orlando Airport
subtitle: A detailed walkthrough of MCO Terminal A, Terminal B, and the new Terminal C - gates, food, shops, and tips.

sections.heading: Terminal A Overview
sections.content: Terminal A at MCO serves as the main hub for many domestic airlines. Airsides 1 and 3 are accessible from Terminal A, serving carriers including American Airlines, Delta, United, and several international airlines. The terminal features a wide selection of dining options including chain restaurants and local eateries.

sections.heading: Terminal B Overview
sections.content: Terminal B is located on the south side of the airport and provides access to Airsides 2 and 4. Southwest Airlines, JetBlue, Spirit, Frontier, and others operate from this terminal. Terminal B has its own food court, shopping options, and a CLEAR enrollment center for expedited security.

sections.heading: Terminal C - The New Addition
sections.content: Terminal C opened in 2022 as MCO's first new terminal in decades. It features 15 gates, automated screening lanes, and modern amenities. JetBlue and Caribbean airlines are among the primary operators. Terminal C is connected to the main terminal complex by an automated people mover system.

sections.heading: Getting Between Terminals
sections.content: The automated people mover connects all terminals at MCO, running continuously. Walking between Terminal A and Terminal B through the main building takes about 10 to 15 minutes. Allow extra time if connecting through Terminal C. BusBrother group pickups can be coordinated at any terminal's ground transportation area.

title: MCO Parking Guide - Orlando Airport Parking Options and Tips
subtitle: Compare parking options at Orlando International Airport - economy, garage, valet, and off-site alternatives.

sections.heading: On-Site Parking Options
sections.content: MCO offers North and South parking garages connected directly to terminals at $19 to $27 per day. Terminal Top parking on the roof level is the most expensive at $27 per day but the most convenient. Levels 3 through 6 in both garages cost $19 per day and still provide covered, walkable access to the terminals.

sections.heading: Economy Parking
sections.content: The economy lots at MCO are located further from the terminals and cost $10 per day. A free shuttle runs continuously between the economy lots and the terminal. Economy parking fills up during holidays and peak travel seasons, so arriving early is recommended. Prepaid parking reservations are available on the MCO website.

sections.heading: Off-Site Parking
sections.content: Several off-site parking companies operate near MCO, offering rates from $5 to $8 per day with shuttle service to the terminal. Popular options include The Parking Spot and similar services. Off-site lots typically offer covered and uncovered options with free shuttle service running every 5 to 10 minutes.

sections.heading: Skip Parking with Group Transportation
sections.content: For groups of 10 or more, BusBrother airport pickup eliminates parking costs entirely. The driver waits at the terminal and loads everyone with their luggage, departing directly to your destination. No walking to garages, no shuttle buses, and no parking fees accumulating while you travel.

subtitle: Everything you need to know about FLL airport - terminals, airlines, cruise port access, and group transportation.

sections.heading: About FLL Airport
sections.content: Fort Lauderdale-Hollywood International Airport (FLL) is the second busiest airport in South Florida, serving over 36 million passengers annually. The airport is located in Dania Beach, between Fort Lauderdale and Hollywood. FLL is a major hub for Spirit Airlines and JetBlue, and its proximity to Port Everglades makes it a top choice for cruise passengers.

sections.heading: Terminal Layout
sections.content: FLL has four terminals with multiple concourses. Terminal 1 serves JetBlue and others, Terminal 2 handles United and others, Terminal 3 serves Delta and others, and Terminal 4 is home to Spirit Airlines and international carriers. A pedestrian skybridge connects Terminal 1 to the Brightline train station.

sections.heading: Cruise Port Connection
sections.content: Port Everglades is literally across the street from FLL, making this airport the most convenient for cruise passengers sailing from Fort Lauderdale. Many cruise lines offer shuttle service between FLL and the port. BusBrother also provides group transfer service between FLL and Port Everglades or any South Florida destination.

sections.heading: Getting to Central Florida
sections.content: FLL is approximately 200 miles south of Orlando. The Brightline train now connects Fort Lauderdale to Orlando in about 3.5 hours. For groups, BusBrother charter bus service provides direct transportation between FLL and Central Florida destinations with plenty of luggage space.

title: FLL Terminals Guide - Navigating Fort Lauderdale Airport
subtitle: A terminal-by-terminal guide to Fort Lauderdale-Hollywood International Airport with airlines, food, and tips.

sections.heading: Terminal 1
sections.content: Terminal 1 at FLL is home to JetBlue Airways, one of the largest carriers at the airport. The terminal features modern amenities including a food court and retail shops. Terminal 1 connects to the Brightline train station via a covered skybridge, making it easy to access rail service to Miami, West Palm Beach, and Orlando.

sections.heading: Terminal 2
sections.content: Terminal 2 serves United Airlines and several other domestic carriers. The terminal has undergone recent renovations with improved seating areas and additional food options. United operates a significant number of flights from Terminal 2 connecting Fort Lauderdale to hubs across the country.

sections.heading: Terminal 3
sections.content: Terminal 3 is home to Delta Air Lines and several other carriers. The terminal features Delta Sky Club lounge access for eligible passengers. Dining options include a mix of quick service and sit-down restaurants. Terminal 3 has convenient access to the parking garage and ground transportation area.

sections.heading: Terminal 4
sections.content: Terminal 4 serves Spirit Airlines, which is the largest carrier at FLL by flight volume, along with several international airlines. The terminal handles many low-cost carrier flights and has a busy international arrivals area with customs and immigration facilities.

title: Orlando Sanford International Airport (SFB) - Complete Guide
subtitle: Everything you need to know about Sanford Airport - a less crowded alternative to MCO with easy access to Central Florida.

sections.heading: About Sanford Airport
sections.content: Orlando Sanford International Airport (SFB) is a smaller alternative to MCO located in Sanford, about 30 miles north of Orlando. The airport primarily serves Allegiant Air, which offers low-cost flights from over 60 small and mid-size US cities. SFB is less crowded and easier to navigate than MCO, making it popular with budget travelers and snowbirds.

sections.heading: Getting to Central Florida from SFB
sections.content: SFB is located off Interstate 4 and SR-417, providing easy access to Orlando, Daytona Beach, and the Space Coast. Orlando attractions are about 30 to 45 minutes south. Port Canaveral is about 50 minutes east. BusBrother provides group shuttle service from SFB to any Central Florida destination.

sections.heading: Parking and Services
sections.content: Parking at SFB is significantly cheaper than MCO, with economy lots starting at $6 per day and close-in lots at $10 per day. The airport has basic food and retail options inside the terminal. Rental cars are available from major agencies on-site.

sections.heading: Who Should Fly into SFB
sections.content: SFB is ideal for groups on a budget, especially those heading to Daytona Beach, the Space Coast, or northern Orlando. Allegiant Air flies from many smaller cities not served by MCO. The lower parking costs and simpler terminal make it a stress-free arrival experience.

title: Palm Beach International Airport (PBI) - Complete Guide
subtitle: Everything you need to know about PBI airport - terminals, airlines, and ground transportation for the Palm Beach area.

sections.heading: About Palm Beach International Airport
sections.content: Palm Beach International Airport (PBI) is located in West Palm Beach, serving the Palm Beach County area and the Treasure Coast. The airport handles about 7 million passengers annually and is served by major carriers including Delta, American, JetBlue, Southwest, and United. PBI is a popular choice for travelers heading to Palm Beach, Jupiter, and Stuart.

sections.heading: Terminal and Airlines
sections.content: PBI has a single terminal with two concourses. Concourse A serves American, United, and others, while Concourse B handles Delta, JetBlue, Southwest, and Spirit. The terminal features recently renovated check-in areas and a food court with several dining options.

sections.heading: Ground Transportation
sections.content: PBI offers rental cars, taxis, rideshare services, and hotel shuttles. The Brightline train station in downtown West Palm Beach is about 4 miles from the airport. BusBrother provides group charter service from PBI to destinations across South and Central Florida.

sections.heading: Connecting to Other Destinations
sections.content: PBI is about 170 miles south of Orlando and 70 miles north of Fort Lauderdale. The airport serves as a convenient entry point for the Treasure Coast and northern Palm Beach County. For groups heading north to Central Florida, BusBrother offers comfortable motorcoach transportation.

title: Daytona Beach International Airport (DAB) - Complete Guide
subtitle: Everything you need to know about DAB airport - airlines, services, and getting to Central Florida attractions.

sections.heading: About Daytona Beach Airport
sections.content: Daytona Beach International Airport (DAB) is a small regional airport serving the Daytona Beach area and northern Volusia County. The airport handles a limited number of flights primarily operated by American Airlines and Delta Air Lines with connections through Charlotte, Atlanta, and Dallas. DAB is a convenient option for travelers heading to Daytona Beach and Daytona International Speedway.

sections.heading: Terminal and Services
sections.content: DAB has a single terminal with a handful of gates. The terminal is easy to navigate with minimal wait times. Rental car agencies are located in the terminal, and parking is available at $10 per day. The airport offers basic food and retail options inside the secure area.

sections.heading: Getting Around from DAB
sections.content: DAB is located about 55 miles northeast of Orlando and 50 miles north of Port Canaveral. I-4 and I-95 provide easy highway access to the rest of Central Florida. BusBrother offers group transportation from DAB to Orlando, Port Canaveral, and other Central Florida destinations.

sections.heading: When to Fly into DAB
sections.content: DAB is a good option when flight prices to MCO are high, especially during Bike Week, the Daytona 500, and spring break. The small airport means faster check-in and pickup. Groups heading to Daytona Beach events benefit from BusBrother service between DAB and area hotels.

title: MCO Ground Transportation - All Options Compared
subtitle: Compare every ground transportation option at Orlando International Airport - rideshare, rental cars, shuttles, buses, and charter service.

sections.heading: Rideshare and Taxis
sections.content: Uber and Lyft operate at MCO with designated pickup areas on Level 2 of the terminal garages. Rides to Walt Disney World cost approximately $25 to $35 and take about 30 minutes. Rides to International Drive cost $15 to $25. Taxis operate from the ground transportation level and charge metered rates that tend to be higher than rideshare.

sections.heading: Rental Cars
sections.content: MCO has a dedicated rental car center connected to the terminals by a people mover. Major companies including Hertz, Avis, Enterprise, Budget, and National operate there. During peak season, rental car rates in Orlando can exceed $100 per day. Booking well in advance is strongly recommended.

sections.heading: Hotel Shuttles
sections.content: Many Orlando area hotels offer complimentary or paid shuttle service to and from MCO. Disney Resort hotels offer Mears Connect service. Universal Orlando hotels offer shuttle service through the resort. Independent shuttle companies also provide shared-ride service to major hotel corridors.

sections.heading: Charter Bus and Group Service
sections.content: For groups of 10 or more, BusBrother charter bus service from MCO is the most cost-effective and convenient option. The driver meets your group at baggage claim, loads all luggage, and drives directly to your destination. No splitting into multiple cars, no surge pricing, and no navigating unfamiliar roads.

sections.heading: Brightline Train
sections.content: The Brightline high-speed train now connects MCO to South Florida with stations in West Palm Beach, Fort Lauderdale, and Miami. The MCO station is inside the airport complex. This is a great option for individual travelers heading to South Florida but less practical for large groups with luggage.

title: FLL Ground Transportation - All Options Compared
subtitle: Compare every ground transportation option at Fort Lauderdale airport - rideshare, rental cars, shuttles, and charter bus service.

sections.heading: Rideshare and Taxis
sections.content: Uber and Lyft operate at FLL with pickup on the upper level of each terminal. Rides to Port Everglades cost only $8 to $12 due to the close proximity. Rides to Miami Beach are about $30 to $45. Taxis are available at designated stands outside each terminal at metered rates.

sections.heading: Rental Cars
sections.content: FLL has a rental car center connected to the terminals by a free shuttle bus. All major rental car companies operate there. Rates in South Florida vary widely by season, with winter months being the most expensive due to snowbird demand.

sections.heading: Brightline Train
sections.content: The Brightline train station at FLL connects via a covered skybridge to Terminal 1. From FLL, you can ride Brightline north to West Palm Beach, south to Miami, or all the way to Orlando International Airport. This is an excellent option for travelers making intercity connections.

sections.heading: Charter Bus for Groups
sections.content: Groups arriving at FLL for cruises, corporate events, or vacations benefit from BusBrother charter service. The driver meets the group at baggage claim and handles all luggage. Direct service to Port Everglades, Miami Beach, Palm Beach, and Central Florida destinations is available.

title: SR-528 Beachline Expressway - Complete Driving Guide
subtitle: Everything you need to know about driving the Beachline Expressway from Orlando to the Space Coast - tolls, exits, traffic patterns, and tips.

sections.heading: About the Beachline Expressway
sections.content: State Road 528, commonly known as the Beachline Expressway, is a 53-mile toll road connecting Orlando to the Space Coast. The highway runs east-west from its interchange with I-4 near downtown Orlando to Cape Canaveral and Cocoa Beach on the Atlantic coast. It is the primary route for cruise passengers heading to Port Canaveral and beachgoers heading to the Space Coast.

sections.heading: Toll Information
sections.content: SR-528 is a toll road with multiple toll plazas along the route. Tolls total approximately $4 to $6 each way depending on entry and exit points. The road accepts SunPass, E-Pass, and toll-by-plate for vehicles without transponders. BusBrother charter buses include all toll costs in the quoted price.

sections.heading: Traffic Patterns
sections.content: Westbound traffic is heaviest on Sunday afternoons and Monday mornings as beachgoers return to Orlando. Eastbound traffic peaks on Friday afternoons and Saturday mornings. Cruise embarkation days, typically Saturdays and Sundays, create additional eastbound traffic near Port Canaveral. Rocket launch days can cause significant congestion near the Space Coast exits.

sections.heading: Key Exits and Landmarks
sections.content: Important exits along SR-528 include the I-4 interchange, the SR-417 connection, the Florida Turnpike junction, the Orlando International Airport exit, and the terminal exits for Port Canaveral. The eastern end of the highway crosses the Banana River and Indian River before reaching the barrier island communities of Cape Canaveral and Cocoa Beach.

title: I-4 Corridor - Complete Driving Guide for Central Florida
subtitle: Everything you need to know about driving Interstate 4 between Daytona Beach, Orlando, and Tampa - construction, traffic, and tips.

sections.heading: About Interstate 4
sections.content: Interstate 4 is the primary east-west highway through Central Florida, running 132 miles from Daytona Beach to Tampa. Despite being labeled an east-west interstate, I-4 actually runs mostly northeast to southwest. The highway passes through Orlando, Sanford, and Lakeland and is one of the most heavily traveled corridors in the southeastern United States.

sections.heading: The I-4 Ultimate Project
sections.content: The I-4 Ultimate project is a massive reconstruction of 21 miles of I-4 through downtown Orlando, adding express toll lanes and rebuilding interchanges. The project has been underway for several years and continues to cause lane shifts, reduced speed zones, and congestion. Travelers should expect delays and plan extra time when driving through the Orlando section of I-4.

sections.heading: Traffic Hotspots
sections.content: The worst congestion on I-4 occurs in the Orlando metropolitan area, particularly near the I-4/SR-408 interchange downtown, the theme park corridor near exits 62 through 68, and the I-4/I-275 interchange in Tampa. Rush hour traffic from 7 to 9 AM and 4 to 7 PM can double travel times through these areas.

sections.heading: Orlando to Tampa Segment
sections.content: The drive from Orlando to Tampa covers about 85 miles and takes 75 to 90 minutes without traffic. The route passes through Lakeland and Plant City. This segment is popular with cruise passengers heading to Port Tampa Bay. BusBrother charter service from Orlando to Tampa lets your group avoid the stress of I-4 traffic entirely.

sections.heading: Orlando to Daytona Beach Segment
sections.content: The drive from Orlando to Daytona Beach covers about 55 miles via I-4 to I-95 North, taking 50 to 70 minutes. This route passes through Sanford and DeLand. The segment is heavily used during Daytona 500 weekend, Bike Week, and spring break. BusBrother provides group transportation to Daytona Beach events.

title: I-95 Central Florida - Complete Driving Guide
subtitle: Everything you need to know about driving I-95 through Central Florida - exits, traffic, and connections to the coast.

sections.heading: About I-95 in Central Florida
sections.content: Interstate 95 is the main north-south highway along Florida's east coast, running from Jacksonville to Miami. Through Central Florida, I-95 passes through Volusia, Brevard, Indian River, and St. Lucie counties. The highway provides direct access to Daytona Beach, Titusville, Melbourne, Vero Beach, and Fort Pierce.

sections.heading: Key Interchanges
sections.content: Important interchanges along I-95 in Central Florida include the I-4 junction near Daytona Beach, the SR-528 Beachline Expressway connection near Cocoa, the SR-520 exit for Cocoa Beach and Port Canaveral, and the SR-60 exit for Vero Beach. The I-95/SR-528 interchange is the primary connection between the interstate and Port Canaveral.

sections.heading: Traffic and Speed
sections.content: I-95 through Central Florida typically moves at or above the posted speed limit of 70 mph outside of urban areas. Congestion occurs around the Daytona Beach area during events and near Melbourne during rush hours. Construction zones periodically reduce speeds. BusBrother drivers monitor traffic conditions in real time.

sections.heading: Connecting to Destinations
sections.content: From I-95, travelers can reach Port Canaveral via SR-528 (about 15 minutes from the interchange), Daytona Beach via US-92 or I-4, and the Kennedy Space Center via SR-405. BusBrother uses I-95 for groups traveling from Jacksonville, Daytona Beach, or South Florida to Space Coast destinations.

quickFacts: Key exit: SR-528 for Port Canaveral
Passes through: Daytona to Vero Beach

title: US-192 Irlo Bronson Memorial Highway - Kissimmee Driving Guide
subtitle: Navigate US-192 through Kissimmee - the gateway to Walt Disney World with hotels, restaurants, and attractions along the corridor.

sections.heading: About US-192
sections.content: US-192, also known as Irlo Bronson Memorial Highway, is a major east-west route through Kissimmee and the southern Orlando tourist corridor. The highway runs from Four Corners near Clermont east through Kissimmee to Melbourne on the coast. The western section near Walt Disney World is lined with hotels, restaurants, and tourist attractions, making it one of the busiest commercial corridors in Osceola County.

sections.heading: The Tourist Corridor
sections.content: The stretch of US-192 between I-4 and the Walt Disney World entrance is known as the tourist corridor. This section features hundreds of hotels, vacation rental offices, souvenir shops, dinner shows like Medieval Times and Capone's, and family attractions. Traffic is heavy year-round, especially during theme park operating hours.

sections.heading: Getting to Disney from US-192
sections.content: US-192 intersects with World Drive, the main entrance to Walt Disney World, near Mile Marker 8. Hotels along US-192 offer some of the most affordable rates in the Disney area. The drive from the US-192 corridor to Magic Kingdom takes about 15 to 25 minutes depending on traffic and which park gate you are entering.

sections.heading: Group Travel on US-192
sections.content: BusBrother picks up groups from US-192 corridor hotels for transportation to theme parks, Port Canaveral, Orlando International Airport, and other destinations. The corridor is popular with family reunions and youth sports teams staying in the budget-friendly hotels and vacation homes along this route.

quickFacts: Also known as: Irlo Bronson Memorial Highway

title: Florida Turnpike - Central Florida Driving Guide
subtitle: Everything you need to know about driving the Florida Turnpike through Central Florida - tolls, exits, and connections to major destinations.

sections.heading: About the Florida Turnpike
sections.content: The Florida Turnpike is a 312-mile toll highway running from Interstate 75 in Wildwood south to Homestead near Miami. Through Central Florida, the Turnpike passes through Osceola and Orange counties, providing a north-south alternative to I-95 and I-75. The highway is one of the most traveled toll roads in the country.

sections.heading: Toll Information
sections.content: The Florida Turnpike uses electronic tolling with SunPass transponders and toll-by-plate for vehicles without transponders. Toll-by-plate adds a surcharge to the base toll. From Orlando to Fort Lauderdale, tolls total approximately $20 to $25 each way. BusBrother charter service includes all tolls in the quoted price.

sections.heading: Central Florida Exits
sections.content: Key Central Florida exits include the I-4 interchange in Orlando, the SR-528 (Beachline) connection for Port Canaveral, the US-192 exit for Kissimmee and Disney, and the SR-429 connector for western Orange County. The Turnpike is the fastest route from Orlando to South Florida.

sections.heading: Orlando to South Florida
sections.content: The drive from Orlando to Fort Lauderdale via the Florida Turnpike covers approximately 200 miles and takes about 3 to 3.5 hours. This is the primary route for cruise passengers heading to Port Everglades. BusBrother provides comfortable group charter service along this corridor.

title: US-1 Space Coast Driving Guide
subtitle: Navigate US Highway 1 along the Space Coast - from Titusville through Cocoa and Melbourne with scenic stops and local tips.

sections.heading: About US-1 on the Space Coast
sections.content: US Highway 1 runs along the Indian River Lagoon through the Space Coast communities of Titusville, Cocoa, Rockledge, Melbourne, and Palm Bay. This historic highway predates the interstate system and passes through the downtown areas of each town. The route offers a more scenic alternative to I-95 for travelers who have extra time.

sections.heading: Key Stops Along the Way
sections.content: Points of interest along US-1 on the Space Coast include historic downtown Titusville with its space-themed murals, Cocoa Village with its boutique shops and restaurants, the Melbourne Arts District, and numerous waterfront parks along the Indian River. The highway also provides access to the Kennedy Space Center Visitor Complex.

sections.heading: Driving Conditions
sections.content: US-1 is a surface road with traffic lights and speed limits ranging from 35 to 55 mph depending on the area. Travel along US-1 is slower than I-95 but offers access to local businesses, restaurants, and attractions. During rocket launches, traffic near Titusville can be extremely heavy.

sections.heading: Group Excursions
sections.content: BusBrother uses US-1 for scenic group tours of the Space Coast, including stops at launch viewing areas, Cocoa Village, and waterfront parks. The route is ideal for groups wanting a relaxed, scenic experience rather than a highway-speed transit.

title: SR-417 Central Florida GreeneWay - Driving Guide
subtitle: Navigate the GreeneWay toll road around eastern Orlando - connections to MCO, Port Canaveral, and the Space Coast.

sections.heading: About the GreeneWay
sections.content: State Road 417, the Central Florida GreeneWay, is a 55-mile toll road forming part of the beltway around Orlando. It runs from Sanford in the north to the Osceola County line in the south, passing near Orlando International Airport. The GreeneWay is a faster alternative to I-4 for many east-side destinations.

sections.heading: Airport and Port Connections
sections.content: SR-417 connects to SR-528 (Beachline) for access to MCO and Port Canaveral. The interchange provides a seamless connection between the GreeneWay and the Space Coast corridor. For groups heading from northern Orlando or Sanford to Port Canaveral, SR-417 to SR-528 is often the fastest route.

sections.heading: Toll Information
sections.content: SR-417 uses electronic tolling through the Central Florida Expressway Authority. SunPass and E-Pass transponders are accepted. Toll-by-plate is available for vehicles without transponders. Tolls vary by distance traveled but are generally moderate for the time savings provided.

sections.heading: Avoiding I-4 Traffic
sections.content: SR-417 serves as a bypass around the congested I-4 corridor through downtown Orlando. Groups traveling from north Orlando to the Space Coast or southern Orange County save significant time by using SR-417 instead of fighting I-4 traffic. BusBrother drivers use SR-417 regularly for efficient routing.

quickFacts: Connects to: SR-528 for MCO/Port Canaveral

title: SR-408 East-West Expressway - Orlando Driving Guide
subtitle: Navigate the East-West Expressway through downtown Orlando - connections, tolls, and traffic tips.

sections.heading: About the East-West Expressway
sections.content: State Road 408 is a 22-mile toll road running through the heart of Orlando from Ocoee in the west to the SR-50/SR-520 junction in the east. The highway passes directly through downtown Orlando and provides critical connections between the western suburbs and the eastern communities.

sections.heading: Key Connections
sections.content: SR-408 connects to I-4 in downtown Orlando, SR-417 (GreeneWay) on the east side, and SR-429 (Western Beltway) on the west side. The highway provides access to downtown Orlando, the Amway Center, Camping World Stadium, and the Florida Citrus Bowl area.

sections.heading: Traffic Patterns
sections.content: Rush hour traffic on SR-408 is heaviest between 7 and 9 AM eastbound and 4 and 7 PM westbound. The downtown section near I-4 experiences the most congestion. During events at the Amway Center or Camping World Stadium, expect additional delays near the downtown exits.

sections.heading: Toll Information
sections.content: SR-408 uses electronic tolling with SunPass and E-Pass. Toll-by-plate is available for cash-free travel. The total toll from end to end is approximately $3 to $4. BusBrother uses SR-408 for efficient routing through the Orlando metro area.

title: I-275 Tampa Bay Area - Complete Driving Guide
subtitle: Navigate Interstate 275 through Tampa and St. Petersburg - bridges, traffic patterns, and cruise port access.

sections.heading: About I-275
sections.content: Interstate 275 is the primary highway through the Tampa Bay metropolitan area, running 59 miles from its southern junction with I-75 in Palmetto through St. Petersburg, across Tampa Bay, through downtown Tampa, and north to its reunion with I-75 near Wesley Chapel. The highway crosses Tampa Bay twice, on the Howard Frankland Bridge and the approach to the Sunshine Skyway Bridge corridor.

sections.heading: Howard Frankland Bridge
sections.content: The Howard Frankland Bridge carries I-275 across Old Tampa Bay between Tampa and St. Petersburg. The bridge is one of the most heavily traveled segments of highway in the Tampa Bay area. A new span is under construction to add capacity and replace the aging westbound structure. Delays are common during rush hours and construction periods.

sections.heading: Cruise Port Access
sections.content: Port Tampa Bay is accessible from I-275 via the downtown Tampa exits. The Channelside Drive exit leads directly to the cruise terminal area. From I-4, drivers connect to I-275 South and follow signs to the port. BusBrother navigates this route regularly for cruise shuttle groups.

sections.heading: Traffic Tips
sections.content: I-275 through Tampa experiences heavy congestion during morning and evening rush hours, particularly on the Howard Frankland Bridge and through the downtown interchange with I-4. The Gandy Boulevard exit area is also a frequent bottleneck. BusBrother drivers time departures to avoid peak traffic windows.

title: Howard Frankland Bridge - Tampa Bay Crossing Guide
subtitle: Everything you need to know about crossing the Howard Frankland Bridge between Tampa and St. Petersburg - construction updates and alternatives.

sections.heading: About the Bridge
sections.content: The Howard Frankland Bridge carries Interstate 275 across Old Tampa Bay, connecting Tampa to St. Petersburg. The bridge spans approximately 4.5 miles and is one of three bridges crossing Tampa Bay. Daily traffic exceeds 175,000 vehicles, making it one of the busiest bridges in Florida.

sections.heading: New Bridge Construction
sections.content: A new Howard Frankland Bridge is under construction adjacent to the existing spans. The new bridge will carry westbound traffic and include a dedicated express bus and bicycle lane. Construction has caused periodic lane closures and reduced speed limits on the existing bridge.

sections.heading: Alternatives
sections.content: If the Howard Frankland is backed up, the Courtney Campbell Causeway (SR-60) runs parallel about 4 miles to the north and connects Tampa to Clearwater. The Gandy Bridge (US-92) is another alternative about 4 miles to the south. BusBrother drivers monitor bridge conditions and use the fastest available crossing.

sections.heading: Best Times to Cross
sections.content: The Howard Frankland is most congested during weekday rush hours from 7 to 9 AM westbound and 4 to 7 PM eastbound. Weekend traffic is generally lighter but can be heavy during major events at Raymond James Stadium or Tropicana Field.

title: Courtney Campbell Causeway - Tampa to Clearwater Guide
subtitle: Drive the scenic Courtney Campbell Causeway between Tampa and Clearwater - with parks, kayak launches, and bay views.

sections.heading: About the Causeway
sections.content: The Courtney Campbell Causeway (SR-60) connects Tampa to Clearwater across upper Tampa Bay. The 9.5-mile causeway features a multi-use trail, fishing areas, and kayak launch points along its length. It is both a transportation route and a recreational destination, with dedicated parking areas for trail users and anglers.

sections.heading: Scenic Drive
sections.content: The causeway offers panoramic views of Tampa Bay with dolphins and manatees frequently spotted in the surrounding waters. The Ben T. Davis Beach on the Tampa side is a popular beach park. The Courtney Campbell Trail runs alongside the road for the entire length, popular with cyclists and joggers.

sections.heading: As an Alternative Route
sections.content: When the Howard Frankland Bridge is congested, the Courtney Campbell Causeway serves as a northern alternative. The drive from Tampa to Clearwater takes about 20 to 25 minutes on the causeway. BusBrother uses this route for groups heading to Clearwater Beach, Clearwater Marine Aquarium, or the Gulf beaches.

sections.heading: Nearby Attractions
sections.content: The Clearwater end of the causeway is close to Clearwater Beach, consistently ranked as one of the best beaches in the United States. The Tampa end connects to the Westshore business district and Tampa International Airport. BusBrother provides group transportation along this scenic corridor.

title: SR-A1A Coastal Highway - Florida's Scenic Atlantic Drive
subtitle: Drive the iconic A1A along Florida's Atlantic coast - beaches, surf towns, and coastal communities from Daytona to Fort Lauderdale.

sections.heading: About A1A
sections.content: State Road A1A is Florida's iconic coastal highway running along the Atlantic Ocean barrier islands. The road connects beach communities from Fernandina Beach in the north to Key West in the south, though it is not continuous. Through Central Florida, A1A passes through Daytona Beach, New Smyrna Beach, Cocoa Beach, Indialantic, Melbourne Beach, and Vero Beach.

sections.heading: Space Coast Section
sections.content: The A1A segment through the Space Coast passes Cape Canaveral, Cocoa Beach, Patrick Space Force Base, Satellite Beach, and Indialantic. This section includes the famous Cocoa Beach Pier, Ron Jon Surf Shop, and numerous oceanfront hotels and restaurants. The road offers direct views of rocket launches from Kennedy Space Center and Cape Canaveral Space Force Station.

sections.heading: Daytona Beach Section
sections.content: A1A through Daytona Beach passes along the famous hard-packed sand beach where cars are allowed to drive on the sand in designated areas. The road runs past the Daytona Beach Boardwalk, the Bandshell, and Ponce de Leon Inlet Lighthouse. New Smyrna Beach to the south is known as one of the best surf spots on the East Coast.

sections.heading: Group Coastal Tours
sections.content: BusBrother offers scenic coastal tours along A1A for groups wanting to experience Florida's beach culture. Popular routes include Orlando to Cocoa Beach, Daytona Beach tours, and multi-stop coastal excursions. The slow pace of A1A makes it ideal for sightseeing rather than point-to-point transportation.

title: US-27 Central Florida Driving Guide
subtitle: Navigate US Highway 27 through Central Florida - the inland corridor connecting Clermont, Haines City, and Lake Wales.

sections.heading: About US-27 in Central Florida
sections.content: US Highway 27 runs through the heart of Central Florida's lake country, passing through Clermont, Minneola, Haines City, Lake Wales, and Sebring. The highway offers a scenic drive through rolling hills and citrus groves. The area is rapidly developing with new residential communities and retail centers.

sections.heading: Key Destinations
sections.content: Along US-27 in Central Florida, travelers find LEGOLAND Florida Resort near Winter Haven, Bok Tower Gardens in Lake Wales, and numerous small-town downtown areas. The Clermont section provides access to the South Lake Trail and the Florida Citrus Tower. The highway is also a major corridor for accessing the Green Swamp wilderness area.

sections.heading: Vacation Home Corridor
sections.content: The US-27 corridor near Four Corners and Champions Gate is one of the densest vacation home areas in Central Florida. Thousands of short-term rental properties serve families visiting nearby Walt Disney World and other theme parks. BusBrother picks up groups from these vacation home communities regularly.

sections.heading: Driving Conditions
sections.content: US-27 through Central Florida is a divided highway with traffic lights at major intersections. Speed limits range from 45 to 65 mph. The highway can be congested near the I-4/US-27 interchange and through Clermont. Early morning fog is common in the lake-dotted areas.

title: I-75 Tampa Corridor - Driving Guide
subtitle: Navigate I-75 around the Tampa Bay area - connections to Sarasota, Fort Myers, and the Gulf Coast.

sections.heading: About I-75 Near Tampa
sections.content: Interstate 75 runs along the eastern edge of the Tampa Bay metropolitan area, providing north-south connectivity from Ocala through Tampa to Fort Myers and Naples. In the Tampa area, I-75 passes through Temple Terrace, Brandon, and Riverview. The highway serves as the main route between Tampa and Southwest Florida destinations.

sections.heading: Key Interchanges
sections.content: Important I-75 interchanges in the Tampa area include I-4 East to Orlando, I-275 South to downtown Tampa and St. Petersburg, SR-56 to Wesley Chapel and New Tampa, and SR-64 to Bradenton. The I-75/I-4 interchange in Hillsborough County is a critical junction for travelers moving between coasts.

sections.heading: Alligator Alley Connection
sections.content: South of the Tampa area, I-75 becomes Alligator Alley as it crosses the Everglades between Naples and Fort Lauderdale. This 78-mile stretch through the Everglades has no services or exits. Tolls apply on Alligator Alley. BusBrother uses this route for groups traveling between Southwest Florida and the Fort Lauderdale cruise port.

sections.heading: Group Travel Corridor
sections.content: BusBrother provides charter service along the I-75 corridor connecting Tampa, Sarasota, Fort Myers, and Naples to Central Florida destinations. The route is especially popular for corporate groups, snowbird communities, and wedding parties traveling between Gulf Coast hotels and event venues.

title: Sawgrass Expressway - South Florida Driving Guide
subtitle: Navigate the Sawgrass Expressway in Broward County - connections to Sawgrass Mills, Fort Lauderdale, and the cruise port.

sections.heading: About the Sawgrass Expressway
sections.content: The Sawgrass Expressway (SR-869) is a 23-mile toll road in western Broward County running from I-75 (Alligator Alley) in the south to the Palmetto Park Road area in the north. The highway provides an efficient north-south route through western Fort Lauderdale and Coral Springs, avoiding the congestion of I-95 and the Turnpike.

sections.heading: Key Connections
sections.content: The Sawgrass Expressway connects to I-75 for access to Naples and Tampa, I-595 for access to Port Everglades and Fort Lauderdale Airport, and I-95 via several east-west arteries. The highway passes near Sawgrass Mills, one of the largest outlet malls in the United States.

sections.heading: Toll Information
sections.content: The Sawgrass Expressway uses electronic tolling with SunPass and toll-by-plate. Tolls vary by distance but are generally $1 to $3 for typical trips. The highway moves quickly with limited congestion outside of rush hours. BusBrother includes all toll costs in charter quotes.

sections.heading: Shopping Group Trips
sections.content: Sawgrass Mills mall near the expressway attracts shopping groups from across Florida. BusBrother provides charter service for groups making day trips from Orlando or Tampa to Sawgrass Mills, handling the drive so the group can focus on shopping.

title: Canaveral National Seashore - Complete Visitor Guide
subtitle: Explore 24 miles of undeveloped Atlantic beach - wildlife, sea turtles, backcountry trails, and group visit planning.

sections.heading: About Canaveral National Seashore
sections.content: Canaveral National Seashore protects 24 miles of undeveloped barrier island beach along the Atlantic Ocean in Brevard and Volusia counties. It is the longest stretch of undeveloped beach on Florida's east coast. The park encompasses over 57,000 acres including the beach, Mosquito Lagoon, and coastal hammock forest. The seashore is managed by the National Park Service.

sections.heading: Playalinda Beach
sections.content: Playalinda Beach is the southern access point, reached from Titusville via SR-406. This section offers 13 parking areas along the beach road with boardwalk access to the sand. Playalinda is famous for its proximity to Kennedy Space Center launch pads, which are visible from the beach. The beach closes during rocket launches for safety.

sections.heading: Apollo Beach
sections.content: Apollo Beach is the northern access point, reached from New Smyrna Beach via A1A. This section offers a visitor center, backcountry trails, and access to Turtle Mound, a 35-foot-tall Native American shell mound dating back over 1,000 years. The trails through the coastal hammock provide excellent birdwatching opportunities.

sections.heading: Wildlife and Sea Turtles
sections.content: Canaveral National Seashore is one of the most important sea turtle nesting areas in the United States. During nesting season from May through October, loggerhead, green, and leatherback turtles come ashore to lay eggs. The park offers ranger-led turtle walks during summer months. The lagoon side supports manatees, dolphins, and over 300 bird species.

sections.heading: Group Visits
sections.content: BusBrother transports groups to Canaveral National Seashore from Orlando and other Central Florida locations. The park is ideal for school field trips, nature groups, photography clubs, and eco-tourism outings. Group parking is available at both the Playalinda and Apollo beach access points.

title: Blue Spring State Park - Complete Visitor Guide
subtitle: Visit Florida's premier manatee viewing destination - crystal-clear springs, hiking, and group excursion planning.

sections.heading: About Blue Spring State Park
sections.content: Blue Spring State Park is located in Orange City, about 30 miles north of Orlando. The park is centered around a first-magnitude spring that pumps 104 million gallons of 72-degree water daily into the St. Johns River. The spring run is one of the most important winter refuges for the endangered Florida manatee, with hundreds gathering in the warm water from November through March.

sections.heading: Manatee Season
sections.content: From November through March, the spring run is closed to swimming, kayaking, and paddleboarding to protect the manatees. During this time, visitors view manatees from the boardwalk along the spring run. On cold days, the park can host 200 to 500 manatees. A webcam and daily manatee count are available on the park website.

sections.heading: Summer Activities
sections.content: From April through October, the spring run opens for swimming and snorkeling in crystal-clear 72-degree water. Kayaking and paddleboarding are also available. The park has a swimming area with a sandy beach near the spring head. Rental kayaks and paddleboards are available from the park concessionaire.

sections.heading: Trails and Nature
sections.content: Blue Spring has several hiking trails including the Pine Island Trail, a 4.5-mile loop through pine flatwoods and hardwood hammock. The park also features the Thursby House, a restored 1872 homestead. Birdwatching is excellent throughout the park, with species including bald eagles, barred owls, and pileated woodpeckers.

sections.heading: Group Visits
sections.content: BusBrother provides group transportation to Blue Spring State Park from Orlando, Daytona Beach, and other Central Florida locations. The park is popular for school field trips, senior groups, and eco-tourism outings. Arrive early during manatee season as the park often fills to capacity by mid-morning on weekends.

title: Wekiwa Springs State Park - Complete Visitor Guide
subtitle: Discover natural springs just 20 minutes from downtown Orlando - swimming, kayaking, hiking, and group outings.

sections.heading: About Wekiwa Springs
sections.content: Wekiwa Springs State Park is a 7,800-acre natural oasis located in Apopka, just 20 minutes north of downtown Orlando. The park features a crystal-clear spring that maintains a constant 72-degree temperature year-round. Despite its proximity to the Orlando urban area, the park protects significant wildlife habitat including black bears, deer, and river otters.

sections.heading: Swimming and Paddling
sections.content: The main spring is a popular swimming hole with a sandy bottom and clear blue-green water. A concession area rents kayaks and canoes for paddling down the Wekiva River, a designated Florida Outstanding Waterway. The river paddle offers chances to see wildlife including turtles, herons, and occasionally alligators and otters.

sections.heading: Hiking and Biking
sections.content: The park offers 13 miles of hiking trails through sand pine scrub, pine flatwoods, and hydric hammock ecosystems. The trails range from easy nature walks to longer backcountry loops. Mountain biking is allowed on designated trails. The park connects to the Wekiva Trail system for extended hiking options.

sections.heading: Group Visits
sections.content: BusBrother transports groups to Wekiwa Springs from Orlando hotels, convention centers, and schools. The park is an excellent choice for corporate team building, school field trips, and youth group outings. The pavilion area can be reserved for group events with grills and picnic facilities available.

title: De Leon Springs State Park - Complete Visitor Guide
subtitle: Swim in a historic spring and make your own pancakes at the Old Spanish Sugar Mill - a unique Florida day trip.

sections.heading: About De Leon Springs
sections.content: De Leon Springs State Park is located in De Leon Springs, about 30 miles north of Orlando near DeLand. The park features a large spring pool that was once used to power a sugar mill in the 1800s. The spring pumps millions of gallons of 72-degree water daily and the pool is open for swimming year-round.

sections.heading: The Old Spanish Sugar Mill
sections.content: The most unique feature of De Leon Springs is the Old Spanish Sugar Mill restaurant, where guests cook their own pancakes on griddles built into each table. The restaurant serves stone-ground pancakes with options including blueberry, banana, and chocolate chip. Waits can exceed an hour on weekends, so arrive early.

sections.heading: Swimming and Kayaking
sections.content: The spring pool is large enough for swimming laps and features a designated swimming area. Kayak and canoe rentals are available for paddling the spring run to Lake Woodruff. The paddle trail offers a peaceful journey through cypress-lined waterways with abundant bird life.

sections.heading: Group Outings
sections.content: BusBrother brings groups to De Leon Springs for unique day trip experiences. The combination of spring swimming and the pancake restaurant makes this a memorable group outing. School groups, church groups, and corporate teams enjoy the interactive dining experience and natural beauty.

title: Crystal River Manatee Swimming Guide
subtitle: The only place in Florida where you can legally swim with wild manatees - tours, seasons, rules, and group trip planning.

sections.heading: Swimming with Manatees
sections.content: Crystal River in Citrus County is the only place in the United States where it is legal to swim with wild manatees in their natural habitat. The warm spring-fed waters of Kings Bay and the Crystal River attract hundreds of manatees during winter months. Licensed tour operators provide guided snorkeling experiences where visitors can observe manatees up close.

sections.heading: Best Time to Visit
sections.content: Manatee season in Crystal River runs from November through March, with peak numbers typically occurring in January and February when Gulf waters are coldest. Water temperatures in the springs remain a constant 72 degrees, attracting manatees from the cooling Gulf. Early morning tours offer the best experiences as manatees are most active at dawn.

sections.heading: Tour Operators and Rules
sections.content: Several licensed tour operators in Crystal River provide manatee snorkeling experiences. Tours typically last 3 to 4 hours and include wetsuit rental, snorkel equipment, and boat transportation to manatee areas. Strict rules prohibit touching, chasing, or feeding manatees. Passive observation where the manatee approaches you is the standard practice.

sections.heading: Three Sisters Springs
sections.content: Three Sisters Springs is a Crystal River National Wildlife Refuge unit that provides a boardwalk viewing area for those who prefer to observe from above. During peak season, in-water access may be restricted on busy days. The springs are incredibly clear and offer stunning views of manatees resting in the warm water.

sections.heading: Group Transportation
sections.content: Crystal River is about 80 miles northwest of Orlando, roughly a 90-minute drive. BusBrother provides group charter service from Orlando, Tampa, and other Central Florida locations to Crystal River. The trip makes an excellent day excursion or overnight group outing.

title: Bok Tower Gardens - Complete Visitor Guide
subtitle: Explore one of Florida's most beautiful gardens - the historic Singing Tower, nature trails, and peaceful grounds in Lake Wales.

sections.heading: About Bok Tower Gardens
sections.content: Bok Tower Gardens is a 250-acre garden and bird sanctuary in Lake Wales, about 60 miles south of Orlando. The centerpiece is the 205-foot neo-Gothic Singing Tower, which houses a 60-bell carillon that plays daily concerts. The gardens were designed by Frederick Law Olmsted Jr. and dedicated by President Calvin Coolidge in 1929.

sections.heading: The Singing Tower and Carillon
sections.content: The Singing Tower is clad in pink and gray Georgia marble with ornate Art Deco carvings. The 60-bell carillon plays automated concerts at 1:00 PM and 3:00 PM daily, with live recitals on select days. Visitors can sit on the surrounding lawn and listen to the bells echo across Iron Mountain, one of the highest points on the Florida peninsula at 298 feet above sea level.

sections.heading: Gardens and Trails
sections.content: The gardens feature over 1,000 species of plants arranged in themed garden rooms including a reflection pool, fern grotto, and endangered plant garden. Nature trails wind through pine woodland and scrub habitat. The Pinewood Estate, a 1930s Mediterranean-style mansion, is open for tours with a separate admission fee.

sections.heading: Group Visits
sections.content: BusBrother transports groups to Bok Tower Gardens for garden club outings, school field trips, and senior group day trips. The gardens offer group rates for parties of 15 or more. The peaceful setting makes it ideal for nature lovers, photographers, and anyone seeking a break from theme park crowds.

title: Homosassa Springs Wildlife State Park - Complete Visitor Guide
subtitle: See native Florida wildlife including manatees, black bears, and the famous hippo Lu - a unique nature experience.

sections.heading: About Homosassa Springs
sections.content: Homosassa Springs Wildlife State Park is located in Homosassa, about 75 miles northwest of Orlando. The park features a natural spring that serves as a rehabilitation and education center for native Florida wildlife. The most famous resident is Lu, a hippopotamus who has lived at the park since 1964 and was granted honorary Florida citizenship by the governor.

sections.heading: The Underwater Observatory
sections.content: The floating underwater observatory allows visitors to descend below the surface of the spring to watch manatees, fish, and other aquatic life through large windows. During winter months, wild manatees enter the spring from the Homosassa River, mixing with the resident rehabilitated manatees. It is one of the most unique wildlife viewing experiences in Florida.

sections.heading: Wildlife Exhibits
sections.content: The park houses native Florida animals in natural habitats including black bears, Florida panthers, bobcats, white-tailed deer, and American alligators. A large aviary houses native bird species. Daily programs include animal encounters, feeding demonstrations, and educational presentations by park staff.

sections.heading: Getting There
sections.content: Homosassa Springs is about 75 miles from Orlando via the Florida Turnpike and US-19. The drive takes approximately 90 minutes. BusBrother provides group charter service from Orlando and Tampa for day trips to Homosassa Springs, often combined with a visit to nearby Crystal River for manatee viewing.

title: Merritt Island National Wildlife Refuge - Complete Visitor Guide
subtitle: Explore 140,000 acres of protected habitat next to Kennedy Space Center - birdwatching, fishing, and guided drives.

sections.heading: About the Refuge
sections.content: Merritt Island National Wildlife Refuge encompasses 140,000 acres of coastal and estuarine habitat on the Space Coast. The refuge shares a boundary with Kennedy Space Center and is managed by the US Fish and Wildlife Service. It is one of the most biologically diverse refuges in the entire National Wildlife Refuge system, home to over 1,500 species of plants and animals.

sections.heading: Black Point Wildlife Drive
sections.content: The 7-mile Black Point Wildlife Drive is a one-way loop road through marsh and impoundment habitats. The drive offers excellent birdwatching opportunities with frequent sightings of roseate spoonbills, wood storks, bald eagles, and various herons and egrets. The drive is open from sunrise to sunset and is especially productive in early morning.

sections.heading: Birdwatching
sections.content: Merritt Island is one of the top birdwatching destinations in the eastern United States. Over 330 bird species have been recorded on the refuge. The annual Space Coast Birding and Wildlife Festival draws thousands of birders from around the world. Peak birding season runs from October through April when migratory species are present.

sections.heading: Access and Closures
sections.content: Parts of the refuge close temporarily during rocket launches at Kennedy Space Center for safety. The Visitor Information Center on SR-402 provides current closure information. Admission to the refuge is free. Hunting and fishing are allowed in designated areas with proper permits. BusBrother provides group transportation for birdwatching tours and nature excursions.

title: Ocala National Forest - Complete Visitor Guide
subtitle: Florida's oldest national forest with springs, trails, and wilderness - a natural escape just 90 minutes from Orlando.

sections.heading: About Ocala National Forest
sections.content: Ocala National Forest is the southernmost national forest in the continental United States, covering nearly 390,000 acres in Marion and Lake counties. The forest is located about 90 minutes north of Orlando and features the largest sand pine scrub forest in the world. The forest contains hundreds of lakes, rivers, and springs within its boundaries.

sections.heading: Springs
sections.content: Ocala National Forest is home to several crystal-clear springs including Alexander Springs, Juniper Springs, Silver Glen Springs, and Salt Springs. Each offers swimming, snorkeling, and paddling opportunities in naturally cool 72-degree water. Juniper Springs and Alexander Springs feature popular canoe and kayak runs through pristine forest waterways.

sections.heading: Hiking the Florida Trail
sections.content: The Florida National Scenic Trail passes through Ocala National Forest for approximately 66 miles. This section is one of the most popular segments of the trail, passing through scrub habitat, along lakeshores, and near several springs. Day hikes and multi-day backpacking trips are both possible with numerous trailheads providing access.

sections.heading: Group Recreation
sections.content: BusBrother provides group transportation to Ocala National Forest for camping trips, spring visits, and hiking excursions. The forest is popular with school groups, scout troops, and outdoor recreation clubs. Multiple developed campgrounds offer sites for groups of various sizes.

quickFacts: Springs: Alexander, Juniper, Silver Glen, Salt

title: Myakka River State Park - Complete Visitor Guide
subtitle: Explore one of Florida's oldest and largest state parks - airboat tours, canopy walkway, and vast wild landscapes near Sarasota.

sections.heading: About Myakka River State Park
sections.content: Myakka River State Park covers nearly 58 square miles in Sarasota County, making it one of the largest state parks in Florida. The park protects diverse habitats including wetlands, prairies, hammocks, and pine flatwoods along the Myakka River. Upper Myakka Lake and Lower Myakka Lake are prominent features of the park.

sections.heading: Airboat and Tram Tours
sections.content: The park offers guided airboat tours on Upper Myakka Lake where visitors can see large alligators, wading birds, and other wildlife. Tram tours through the prairie and wetland areas provide a narrated introduction to the park's ecosystems. Both tours are popular and reservations are recommended during peak season.

sections.heading: Canopy Walkway
sections.content: The Myakka Canopy Walkway is a 100-foot-long suspension bridge 25 feet above the forest floor, connected to a 74-foot observation tower. The walkway provides a bird's-eye view of the palm and oak hammock canopy and the surrounding landscape. It is one of the few canopy walkways in the state.

sections.heading: Group Excursions
sections.content: BusBrother provides charter transportation from Tampa, Sarasota, and Orlando to Myakka River State Park. The park is ideal for nature photography groups, school field trips, and eco-tourism excursions. Pavilions and group camping areas are available with advance reservation.

title: Hillsborough River State Park - Complete Visitor Guide
subtitle: Paddle Class II rapids, hike shady trails, and explore a Civil War-era fort just 30 minutes from downtown Tampa.

sections.heading: About the Park
sections.content: Hillsborough River State Park is one of Florida's original state parks, established in 1936. Located in Thonotosassa, about 30 minutes northeast of downtown Tampa, the park protects a stretch of the Hillsborough River that features some of the only rapids in the otherwise flat Florida landscape. The park covers 3,383 acres of river swamp, hammock, and flatwoods.

sections.heading: Paddling and Rapids
sections.content: The Hillsborough River through the park features Class II rapids, which are very rare in Florida. Kayak and canoe rentals are available from the park concession. The rapids section is mild but fun, making it suitable for beginners. Downstream paddling through cypress-lined channels offers a peaceful wilderness experience.

sections.heading: Fort Foster Historic Site
sections.content: Fort Foster is a reconstructed Second Seminole War fort within the park. The fort was originally built in 1836 to guard a bridge over the Hillsborough River. Reenactment events are held periodically. Ranger-led tours explain the history of the Seminole Wars and the fort's role in the conflict.

sections.heading: Group Activities
sections.content: BusBrother transports groups from Tampa and Orlando to Hillsborough River State Park for kayaking outings, team building events, and educational field trips. The park's swimming pool (open seasonally), picnic pavilions, and campground make it versatile for different types of group events.

title: Honeymoon Island State Park - Complete Visitor Guide
subtitle: One of Florida's best Gulf Coast beaches with nature trails, shelling, and ferry service to Caladesi Island.

sections.heading: About Honeymoon Island
sections.content: Honeymoon Island State Park is located in Dunedin, just north of Clearwater on the Gulf Coast. The island offers 4 miles of Gulf beaches, nature trails through virgin slash pine forest, and ferry service to neighboring Caladesi Island. The park is one of the most visited state parks in Florida, attracting over 1 million visitors annually.

sections.heading: Beaches and Shelling
sections.content: The Gulf-side beaches feature white sand and calm, clear water ideal for swimming and snorkeling. The bayside offers excellent shelling, with sea shells washing up on the more sheltered shores. The pet beach on the south end of the island is one of the few state park beaches in Florida that allows dogs.

sections.heading: Caladesi Island Ferry
sections.content: A ferry operates from Honeymoon Island to Caladesi Island State Park, which is accessible only by boat. Caladesi is consistently ranked among the best beaches in America. The ferry runs every 30 minutes during peak season. The island has a nature trail, kayak trail through mangroves, and a cafe.

sections.heading: Group Beach Days
sections.content: BusBrother brings groups from Orlando and Tampa to Honeymoon Island for beach day trips. The park offers pavilion rentals for group events. Combined with the Caladesi Island ferry, it makes for a full-day beach excursion. The park fills to capacity on busy weekends, so early arrival is essential.

title: Fort De Soto Park - Complete Visitor Guide
subtitle: Tampa Bay's award-winning beach park with historic fort, kayaking, fishing, and some of the best sunsets in Florida.

sections.heading: About Fort De Soto Park
sections.content: Fort De Soto Park is a 1,136-acre county park located on five interconnected islands at the southern tip of St. Petersburg. North Beach at Fort De Soto is consistently ranked among the top beaches in the United States by TripAdvisor and Dr. Beach. The park is operated by Pinellas County and offers beaches, trails, fishing piers, and a historic fort.

sections.heading: Beaches
sections.content: The park has two main beaches. North Beach faces Tampa Bay and offers calm, shallow water perfect for families. East Beach faces the Gulf and offers slightly more wave action. Both beaches feature white sand and clear water. The shoreline is excellent for shell collecting, especially at low tide.

sections.heading: Historic Fort and Nature
sections.content: The park is named for Fort De Soto, a Spanish-American War era fort built in 1898. The fort's mortars and battery can still be explored. The park also features 7 miles of paved trails, a dog beach, two fishing piers, and a 2.25-mile kayak trail through mangrove tunnels. Over 300 bird species have been recorded in the park.

sections.heading: Group Visits
sections.content: BusBrother provides group transportation to Fort De Soto Park from Tampa, St. Petersburg, Orlando, and other Central Florida locations. The park offers group picnic shelters with grills and is popular for corporate outings, family reunions, and wedding events. Camping is available for overnight groups.

title: John Pennekamp Coral Reef State Park - Day Trip Guide
subtitle: America's first undersea park in Key Largo - snorkeling, glass-bottom boats, and the famous Christ of the Abyss statue.

sections.heading: About John Pennekamp
sections.content: John Pennekamp Coral Reef State Park in Key Largo was the first undersea park in the United States, established in 1963. The park encompasses approximately 70 nautical square miles of coral reefs, seagrass beds, and mangrove swamps. The underwater park protects a portion of the Florida Reef, the only living barrier coral reef in the continental United States.

sections.heading: Snorkeling and Diving
sections.content: The park offers snorkeling and diving trips to the coral reef by boat. Popular reef sites include the Christ of the Abyss statue, a 9-foot bronze sculpture submerged in 25 feet of water, and the Molasses Reef area. Snorkeling trips depart at 9:00 AM, 12:00 PM, and 3:00 PM daily. Equipment rental is available at the park.

sections.heading: Glass-Bottom Boat Tours
sections.content: For visitors who prefer to stay dry, glass-bottom boat tours provide views of the reef and marine life from above. The tours last approximately 2.5 hours and narrate the ecology of the reef system. This is a popular option for families with young children and seniors.

sections.heading: Day Trip from Central Florida
sections.content: Key Largo is approximately 250 miles south of Orlando, a drive of about 4 to 4.5 hours via the Florida Turnpike and US-1. While it is a long day trip, BusBrother makes it comfortable by handling all the driving. Groups can depart early morning and return the same evening, or combine with an overnight stay in the Keys.

quickFacts: Famous site: Christ of the Abyss

title: Ichetucknee Springs State Park - Complete Visitor Guide
subtitle: Float down one of Florida's most beautiful spring-fed rivers - tubing, swimming, and crystal-clear water in North Central Florida.

sections.heading: About Ichetucknee Springs
sections.content: Ichetucknee Springs State Park in Fort White, about 120 miles north of Orlando, protects a 6-mile stretch of the Ichetucknee River fed by nine named springs. The crystal-clear water maintains a constant 72-degree temperature and flows through a lush cypress and hardwood forest. The river is one of the most popular tubing destinations in the southeastern United States.

sections.heading: Tubing the River
sections.content: Tubing is the main attraction at Ichetucknee. The full tube run from the north entrance to the south exit covers 3.5 miles and takes about 2 to 3 hours. A shorter 1-mile tube run from the midpoint to the south exit takes about 45 minutes. Tube rentals are available from vendors outside the park. During summer weekends, the park limits daily visitors to protect the resource.

sections.heading: Swimming and Snorkeling
sections.content: The headspring area offers swimming and snorkeling in incredibly clear water. Underwater visibility can exceed 50 feet. Snorkelers can see fish, turtles, and the spring vents bubbling from the limestone below. Swimming is available year-round though the spring area is most popular in summer months.

sections.heading: Group Float Trips
sections.content: BusBrother provides group charter service from Orlando, Jacksonville, and Gainesville to Ichetucknee Springs. The park is ideal for youth group outings, corporate team events, and family reunion day trips. Plan to arrive early during summer weekends as the park reaches capacity.

title: Rainbow Springs State Park - Complete Visitor Guide
subtitle: Swim, tube, and kayak in one of Florida's largest springs - gardens, waterfalls, and the Rainbow River.

sections.heading: About Rainbow Springs
sections.content: Rainbow Springs State Park in Dunnellon is centered around the headspring of the Rainbow River, Florida's fourth-largest spring. The spring pumps an estimated 400 to 600 million gallons of clear water daily. The park was once a popular attraction in the mid-1900s and still features the original gardens with azaleas, waterfalls, and ornamental plantings.

sections.heading: Swimming and Tubing
sections.content: The headspring area offers a large swimming area with clear, cool water and a sandy bottom. The Rainbow River downstream is popular for tubing, with a separate tube launch at the park's tubing area on the river. The tube run takes 2 to 3 hours to float to the take-out point. Tube rentals are available from outfitters near the park.

sections.heading: Gardens and Trails
sections.content: The historic gardens at Rainbow Springs feature azalea gardens that bloom spectacularly in spring, man-made waterfalls, and butterfly gardens. Walking trails wind through the gardens and along the spring run. The park is particularly beautiful in March and April when the azaleas are in peak bloom.

sections.heading: Group Trips
sections.content: BusBrother provides charter transportation to Rainbow Springs from Orlando and Tampa. The drive is about 90 minutes from Orlando. The park is popular with garden clubs, senior groups, and tubing outings for youth organizations. Group pavilion rentals are available with advance reservation.

title: Silver Springs State Park - Complete Visitor Guide
subtitle: Florida's oldest tourist attraction with glass-bottom boats, crystal-clear springs, and kayaking on the Silver River.

sections.heading: About Silver Springs
sections.content: Silver Springs State Park in Ocala is one of Florida's oldest and most famous natural attractions. Glass-bottom boat tours have operated here since 1878, making it one of the earliest tourist attractions in the state. The main spring is one of the largest artesian springs in the world, producing about 550 million gallons of crystal-clear water daily.

sections.heading: Glass-Bottom Boats
sections.content: The glass-bottom boat tour remains the signature experience at Silver Springs. The 30-minute tour glides over the spring vents where visibility can reach 80 feet. Riders see fish, turtles, and submerged fossils through the glass panels. The spring was used as a filming location for several Tarzan movies and the television series Sea Hunt.

sections.heading: Kayaking the Silver River
sections.content: Kayak and canoe rentals are available for self-guided paddles down the Silver River. The river flows through dense forest and is home to colonies of rhesus macaque monkeys introduced in the 1930s for a Tarzan film. Paddlers often spot manatees, turtles, birds, and the distinctive monkeys along the riverbanks.

sections.heading: Group Outings
sections.content: BusBrother provides charter transportation to Silver Springs from Orlando, about 75 miles north via I-75 or the Florida Turnpike. The drive takes approximately 90 minutes. The park is excellent for school field trips, nature groups, and senior outings. Group boat tour rates are available with advance booking.

title: Lake Kissimmee State Park - Complete Visitor Guide
subtitle: Experience old Florida with a cow camp living history, nature trails, and wildlife viewing on the Kissimmee prairie.

sections.heading: About Lake Kissimmee State Park
sections.content: Lake Kissimmee State Park is located in Lake Wales, about 60 miles south of Orlando. The park covers 5,930 acres along the shores of lakes Kissimmee, Tiger, and Rosalie. The park preserves examples of scrub, pine flatwoods, and wet prairie habitats. It is one of the best places in Central Florida to experience the landscape as it looked before development.

sections.heading: Cow Camp Living History
sections.content: The park features a living history cow camp that recreates the life of Florida cowmen (called cow hunters) in 1876. On weekends, costumed interpreters demonstrate period activities including cattle handling, cooking over an open fire, and blacksmithing. The cow camp offers a unique look at Florida's ranching heritage.

sections.heading: Wildlife and Trails
sections.content: The park has 13 miles of hiking trails through diverse habitats. Wildlife commonly seen includes white-tailed deer, wild turkey, bald eagles, sandhill cranes, and bobcats. The observation tower near Lake Kissimmee provides panoramic views of the lake and surrounding prairie.

sections.heading: Group Visits
sections.content: BusBrother transports groups to Lake Kissimmee State Park for educational field trips and nature outings. The living history cow camp is particularly popular with school groups. The park's campground and picnic facilities support overnight and day-use group activities.

title: Sebastian Inlet State Park - Complete Visitor Guide
subtitle: Florida's top surf spot with fishing, boating, and beautiful beaches where the Atlantic meets Indian River Lagoon.

sections.heading: About Sebastian Inlet
sections.content: Sebastian Inlet State Park straddles the inlet where the Indian River Lagoon meets the Atlantic Ocean, located about 80 miles southeast of Orlando. The park is famous for having the best surfing on Florida's east coast, with consistent waves breaking on both sides of the inlet jetties. The park covers land on both Brevard and Indian River county sides of the inlet.

sections.heading: Surfing and Beaches
sections.content: Monster Hole and First Peak are the two primary surf breaks at Sebastian Inlet, known throughout the surfing world for producing quality waves. Surfers travel from across the state to ride here. The beaches on both sides of the inlet offer swimming and sunbathing. The south beach is generally calmer and better for families.

sections.heading: Fishing
sections.content: Sebastian Inlet is one of the premier fishing spots on Florida's east coast. The inlet and jetties produce snook, redfish, flounder, and bluefish. The catwalks along the inlet bridge provide accessible fishing platforms. The Indian River Lagoon side offers flats fishing for redfish and spotted seatrout.

sections.heading: Group Trips
sections.content: BusBrother provides group transportation to Sebastian Inlet from Orlando, Melbourne, and Vero Beach. The park is popular with surf clubs, fishing groups, and beach excursion parties. The park features a campground, boat ramps, and the McLarty Treasure Museum documenting the 1715 Spanish treasure fleet that wrecked nearby.

quickFacts: Famous for: Best surfing in FL

title: Anastasia State Park - Complete Visitor Guide
subtitle: Beach camping and nature trails next to historic St. Augustine - a perfect combination of nature and culture.

sections.heading: About Anastasia State Park
sections.content: Anastasia State Park is located on Anastasia Island in St. Augustine, about 100 miles northeast of Orlando. The 1,600-acre park protects a pristine Atlantic beach, tidal marshes, coastal hammock, and an ancient coquina quarry. The park's beach is consistently rated among the best in Florida for its wide, uncrowded shoreline.

sections.heading: Beach and Water Activities
sections.content: The park offers 4 miles of white sand beach along the Atlantic Ocean. Swimming, surfing, bodyboarding, and surf fishing are popular activities. On the lagoon side, kayak and paddleboard rentals are available for exploring the tidal marshes. A designated windsurfing area attracts enthusiasts when conditions are right.

sections.heading: Nature and History
sections.content: Hiking trails wind through coastal hammock and past the coquina quarry where stone was mined to build the Castillo de San Marcos in nearby St. Augustine. The quarry pools are now filled with water and home to wading birds. Over 200 bird species have been recorded in the park.

sections.heading: Day Trip from Orlando
sections.content: Anastasia State Park is about 100 miles from Orlando, a drive of approximately 2 hours via I-95 North. BusBrother combines St. Augustine sightseeing with beach time at Anastasia for group day trips. The park campground is popular for groups wanting an overnight beach camping experience.

title: Space Coast Travel Guide - Brevard County Visitors Guide
subtitle: Everything you need to know about visiting Florida's Space Coast - beaches, launches, attractions, and group transportation.

sections.heading: About the Space Coast
sections.content: Florida's Space Coast encompasses Brevard County on the central Atlantic coast, stretching from Titusville in the north to Palm Bay in the south. The region gets its name from Kennedy Space Center and Cape Canaveral Space Force Station, which have launched every US human spaceflight mission. The area offers 72 miles of beaches, world-class surfing, and unique wildlife viewing alongside its space heritage.

sections.heading: Top Attractions
sections.content: Kennedy Space Center Visitor Complex is the premier attraction, offering exhibits, IMAX theaters, astronaut encounters, and bus tours past launch pads. Other top attractions include the American Space Museum in Titusville, the Brevard Zoo in Melbourne, Cocoa Beach Pier, and Ron Jon Surf Shop. Merritt Island National Wildlife Refuge provides incredible birdwatching and nature photography.

sections.heading: Beaches
sections.content: Space Coast beaches include Cocoa Beach, Satellite Beach, Indialantic, Melbourne Beach, and Playalinda Beach at Canaveral National Seashore. Cocoa Beach is the most developed with restaurants, shops, and nightlife. Playalinda offers pristine, undeveloped shoreline.
